news_summary: Today’s human rights progress highlights global solidarity against racial discrimination, inclusive international collaboration, proactive measures to protect athletes in Japan, and ongoing advancements in cooperation over decades.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés aujourd’hui en matière de droits de l’homme mettent en évidence la solidarité mondiale contre la discrimination raciale, une collaboration internationale inclusive, des mesures proactives visant à protéger les athlètes au Japon, ainsi que les avancées constantes en matière de coopération depuis des décennies.
news_summary_es: Los avances actuales en materia de derechos humanos ponen de relieve la solidaridad mundial contra la discriminación racial, la colaboración internacional inclusiva, las medidas proactivas para proteger a los deportistas en Japón y los continuos avances en la cooperación a lo largo de décadas.

articles.title: UN calls for solidarity and political will to stamp out racial discrimination
articles.summary: Nearly 70 years after South African police fired upon peaceful demonstrators in Sharpeville protesting apartheid-era laws, killing 69, the UN renewed the commitment to work for justice and equality on Monday, marking the International Day for the Elimination of Racial Discrimination.
articles.content:
The crowd gathered outside Sharpeville police station on 21 March 1960 “came armed not with weapons, but with conviction – not to divide society, but to claim their dignity within it,” General Assembly President Annalena Baerbock recalled in her opening remarks.

But the annual commemoration is about much more than one notorious incident, she said, as racism clearly persists in every corner of the world.

Sinister and damaging

“Sometimes it is explicit and vulgar – an obscenity hurled at another, or graffiti scrawled across a wall. Sometimes it is quiet and discreet – masked in bureaucracy and hidden within the ordinary,” said Ms. Baerbock, speaking in the General Assembly Hall.

“But whether loud or silent, it is sinister, it is damaging, and its consequences extend far beyond individuals.”

As UN Secretary-General António Guterres stated in his remarks, racism harms everyone.

Governments falter on policies

“It persists in the damaging consequences of enslavement, colonialism, and oppression,” he said.

Racism also “feeds many of the problems we face today,” including economic, social and political inequalities, as well as discriminatory policies and practices, and conflicts.

Moreover, “many of the solutions to confront it are being weakened as some governments dismantle anti-racist policies and practices and leaders try to rewrite history.”

The UN chief was particularly troubled by how racism and xenophobia are being mainstreamed on digital platforms and in political discourse.

“What might begin with dog whistles – coded messages meant to embolden other bigots – can quickly turn into full-throated hate speech,” he said.

“We know where this road leads: to further injustice, violence, and even worse.”

The solution is solidarity, he said, calling for governments, institutions, businesses and communities to work together to protect the dignity, justice, equality, and rights of every person.

Keep moving forward

UN High Commissioner for Human Rights Volker Türk highlighted how history has shown that movements for greater equality cannot be held back.

He cited the example of Sharpeville but also Ruby Bridges, the African American girl who desegrated schools in the United States; activist Ana Paula Gomes de Oliveira who is seeking justice for Afro-Brazilian mothers who lost their children to police violence, and US civil rights champion Reverend Jesse Jackson who passed away last month.

“These and countless other acts of resistance have changed the face of our world. Today, we owe them nothing less than an all-out effort to safeguard and advance this progress,” he said.

Justice, human rights and courage

Mr. Türk stressed the critical need for political will, including to fight discrimination through laws that are robustly enforced and strengthen accountability for all forms of racial discrimination and hatred.

“Being anti-racist does not mean standing with one group against another. It means standing on the side of human rights and justice – for all,” he said.

US law professor Justin Hansford – a founding Member of the UN Permanent Forum on People of African Descent – spoke of how he has marched in the streets for racial justice with “tear gas in the air, tanks in the road, names like Trayvon Martin, Mike Brown, and George Floyd on my lips.”

He described the 69 people slaughtered in the Sharpeville massacre as martyrs to the cause of democracy and racial justice whose sacrifice echoes today.

“Their courage demonstrated that the world will always be watching until racial justice is no longer a promise deferred but a condition achieved,” he said.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the UN's renewed commitment to fighting racial discrimination, emphasizing solidarity, justice, and equality on an international scale. It references historical and ongoing struggles against racism and calls for political will and robust laws to protect human rights, which has broad societal significance and positive real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: UN renews commitment to fight racial discrimination on Sharpeville anniversary
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – On March 21, 1960, South African police shot peaceful protesters in Sharpeville who were standing against unfair apartheid laws. This tragic event, where 69 people died, is remembered each year on the International Day for the Elimination of Racial Discrimination.

**What happened** – On March 21, 2024, the United Nations held a special event to mark this day. Leaders, including UN Secretary-General António Guterres and other officials, spoke about the ongoing problem of racism worldwide. They reminded people that racism can be obvious, like hateful words or graffiti, or hidden in everyday systems and laws. They also warned that some governments are weakening efforts to fight racism and that online hate speech is increasing.

**Impact** – This event is important because it shows the UN’s strong promise to keep working for justice and equality. It connects past struggles, like the Sharpeville massacre and civil rights movements, to today’s fight against racism. The UN leaders stressed that racism hurts everyone and that real change needs strong laws and cooperation between governments and communities.

**What’s next step** – The UN calls for more political will to enforce anti-racism laws and protect human rights. They want governments, businesses, and people everywhere to work together so that racial justice is not just a goal but a reality. This means standing up against racism in all its forms and making sure everyone is treated fairly.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN marked the Sharpeville anniversary by renewing its call for global unity and action to end racial discrimination through stronger laws and shared commitment.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU renouvelle son engagement contre la discrimination raciale à l’anniversaire de Sharpeville
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le 21 mars 1960, la police sud-africaine a tiré sur des manifestants pacifiques à Sharpeville qui protestaient contre les lois injustes de l’apartheid. Cet événement tragique, où 69 personnes ont été tuées, est commémoré chaque année lors de la Journée internationale pour l’élimination de la discrimination raciale.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 21 mars 2024, l’Organisation des Nations Unies a organisé un événement spécial pour marquer cette journée. Des dirigeants, dont le Secrétaire général António Guterres, ont parlé du problème toujours présent du racisme dans le monde. Ils ont rappelé que le racisme peut être évident, comme des mots haineux ou des graffitis, ou caché dans les systèmes et les lois du quotidien. Ils ont aussi averti que certains gouvernements affaiblissent les efforts pour combattre le racisme et que les discours haineux en ligne augmentent.

**Impact** – Cet événement est important car il montre la forte promesse de l’ONU de continuer à œuvrer pour la justice et l’égalité. Il relie les luttes passées, comme le massacre de Sharpeville et les mouvements des droits civiques, à la lutte actuelle contre le racisme. Les dirigeants de l’ONU ont souligné que le racisme nuit à tous et que le vrai changement nécessite des lois solides et la coopération entre gouvernements et communautés.

**Prochaine étape** – L’ONU appelle à une volonté politique accrue pour appliquer les lois anti-racistes et protéger les droits humains. Elle souhaite que gouvernements, entreprises et citoyens travaillent ensemble pour que la justice raciale ne soit plus un objectif, mais une réalité. Cela signifie s’opposer au racisme sous toutes ses formes et garantir un traitement équitable pour tous.

**Une phrase clé** – L’ONU a marqué l’anniversaire de Sharpeville en renouvelant son appel à l’unité mondiale et à l’action pour mettre fin à la discrimination raciale grâce à des lois renforcées et un engagement partagé.
articles.personality_title_es: ONU renueva compromiso para combatir la discriminación racial en aniversario de Sharpeville
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El 21 de marzo de 1960, la policía sudafricana disparó contra manifestantes pacíficos en Sharpeville que protestaban contra las injustas leyes del apartheid. Este trágico evento, donde murieron 69 personas, se recuerda cada año en el Día Internacional de la Eliminación de la Discriminación Racial.

**Qué pasó** – El 21 de marzo de 2024, las Naciones Unidas realizaron un evento especial para conmemorar este día. Líderes, incluyendo al Secretario General António Guterres, hablaron sobre el problema continuo del racismo en todo el mundo. Recordaron que el racismo puede ser visible, como palabras de odio o grafitis, o estar oculto en los sistemas y leyes diarias. También advirtieron que algunos gobiernos están debilitando los esfuerzos para combatir el racismo y que el discurso de odio en línea está aumentando.

**Impacto** – Este evento es importante porque muestra la fuerte promesa de la ONU de seguir trabajando por la justicia y la igualdad. Conecta las luchas pasadas, como la masacre de Sharpeville y los movimientos por los derechos civiles, con la lucha actual contra el racismo. Los líderes de la ONU enfatizaron que el racismo daña a todos y que el verdadero cambio requiere leyes firmes y colaboración entre gobiernos y comunidades.

**Próximo paso** – La ONU pide más voluntad política para aplicar leyes antirracistas y proteger los derechos humanos. Quieren que gobiernos, empresas y personas trabajen juntos para que la justicia racial no sea solo una meta, sino una realidad. Esto significa enfrentar el racismo en todas sus formas y asegurar que todos sean tratados con justicia.

**Frase clave** – La ONU conmemoró el aniversario de Sharpeville renovando su llamado a la unidad global y a la acción para acabar con la discriminación racial mediante leyes más fuertes y compromiso compartido.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a diverse crowd of faceless, silhouetted figures standing united outside a stylized police station with softly glowing candles and intertwined hands symbolizing solidarity and justice, set against a subdued palette of earth tones and soft blues to evoke dignity, resilience, and the ongoing fight against racial discrimination.

articles.title: A city opens its doors to the world: New York and the United Nations
articles.summary: Born from the ashes of the Second World War, the dream of a more peaceful and fairer world drew nations together in 1945 under a new vision for humanity: the United Nations.
articles.content:
Eight decades on, the aspiration for peace, dignity and equality still resonates as the UN marks 80 years of its existence in New York City – a place now inseparable from the organization’s global identity…but not always destined to be its home.

Hunter (now Lehman) college in the New York borough of The Bronx was one of the UN’s initial temporary headquarters and the location of the first UN Security Council meeting on US soil on 25 March 1946.

Where else has the Security Council met outside its current location?

The basketball gym at Hunter College was refurbished as the UN Security Council chamber in just three weeks. Journalists were accommodated in a converted swimming pool. One of the first issues the Council discussed was Iran.

UN News/Daniel Dickinson Two current-day employees of Lehman College hold a photograph of the UN Security Council from 1946 which once occupied the college's basketball court.

Listen to audio from the first Security Council meeting at Hunter College.

Hunter College was never large enough to accommodate the UN staff needed to run the organization, not to mention the delegates from the then 51 countries or Member States of the United Nations, so a new temporary headquarters was established in a Second World War munitions factory at Lake Success on Long Island.

UN Photo United Nations staff arrive at Lake Success in October 1946.

In 1946 – as is the case today – UN staffers came from a multicultural and international background. Newspaper reports marvelled at the uniqueness of seeing women in saris and men in traditional Thawb robes.

At Lake Success, meetings were recorded and broadcast globally, representing an unprecedented moment in worldwide broadcast history.

UN Photo Eleanor Roosevelt (centre) joins a UN Radio discussion on the International Bill of Rights.

UN Radio was established in 1946 and an early interviewee was Eleanor Roosevelt (centre), a US delegate (and former First Lady of the United States) who was the driving force behind the Universal Declaration of Human Rights.

World Radio Day is marked every 13 February – the day UN Radio first hit the airwaves, 80 years ago this year.

Watch here a UN video profile of Eleanor Roosevelt.

UN Photo/MB A New York company prepares to move the United Nations.

Soon the UN needed more space and a deal was struck to hold meetings of the General Assembly at the former World’s fair site at Flushing Meadows in the New York borough of Queens. The Security Council and other UN activities remained at Lake Success.

Flushing Meadows was cold and windy, and it showed: delegates often wore coats inside. One usher appeared in a padded Mandarin coat, and Indian delegates added wool wraps over their saris. A UN nurse was on hand to treat many colds, proving that even world diplomacy had to brave a New York chill.

Despite the cold, Secretary-General Lie called the building and the surrounding park a powerful symbol of the warmth of the friendship between the UN and its host city.

Listen to Rula Hinedi, the chief of Guided Tours at the UN talk about Flushing Meadows.

A skating arena was converted into the General Assembly Hall which met there until 1950, by which time the UN had expanded to 60 Member States. (Today there are 193).

UN Photo/Albert Fox A UN staff member checks the nameplates for the countries participating in the UN General Assembly at Flushing Meadow.

The logistics behind organizing international meetings of a scale never seen before fell to the staff of the UN Secretariat, the administrative and executive body of the UN which keeps the organization running.

Here, a UN staff member prepares the nameplates for the countries taking part in a UN General Assembly meeting at Flushing Meadow.

UN Photo Press officers at the United Nations edit verbatim reports from UN meetings.

Behind the scenes, hundreds of communication and PR officers worked on making sure the issues debated in the General Assembly and Security Council reached the widest possible audience. Verbatim reports of meetings were compiled by press officers (pictured below) in English and French, the working languages of the UN and then distributed globally.

While the UN continued its work in Flushing Meadow, efforts were stepped up to find a permanent location for the world body.

UN Photo UN Secretary-General Trygve Lie (centre) accepts a gift of $8.5 million in March 1947 for the purchase of land on the East River in Manhattan.

New York City faced competition from Boston, Philadelphia, San Francisco and Fairfield County in the state of Connecticut as well as Westchester County in New York State.

A gift of $8.5 million from the American industrialist and probably the richest man in the world at the time John D Rockefeller, secured the 17-acre site that UN Headquarters currently occupies on the East River in Manhattan.

Here the then UN Secretary-General, Trygve Lie (centre) accepts the gift from John D Rockefeller Jr. (right) with the Mayor of New York City, William O’Dwyer, also in attendance.

UN Photo Work begins in 1947 on clearing the ground for the building of UN Headquarters.

Work began on clearing the site which 300 years previously had been a tobacco field but had most recently served as meat-packing warehouses.

UN Photo/Kari Berggrav Workers wash after a day of construction at UN Headquarters in New York in 1947.

A UN Radio reporter visited the site and interviewed a bystander:

What fascinates you about looking at this?

Bystander: I'm interested in these guys down here digging a hole.

Reporter: What is your feeling as a New Yorker, as an American, as a person who belongs to one of the nations of the United Nations. What is your honest, your candid feeling about the whole thing?

Bystander: I think it's pretty wonderful. I think if the United Nations works, it'll be the most wonderful thing that ever happened to us.

Reporter: I noticed that you qualified your statement. What do you think might make it not work?

Bystander: Only us, only people.

Reporter: That's the best answer I've ever had.

Listen to the interview here.

The construction of UN Headquarters in Manhattan led by an international team of renown architects including Le Corbusier and Oscar Niemeyertook about three years.

Staff began moving into the building in 1951 and when it was finally completed in 1952 there was office space for some 3,000 people.

UN Photo/BG A window cleaner perches precariously on a window on the First Avenue side of the United Nations headquarters building in 1951.

The UN’s relationship with New York City now stretches back 80 years and historian Chris McNickle says he has “no doubt that the United Nations is where it belongs.

“New York City is the great immigrant city of the world. It's a statement that the city makes that people from all walks of life, from all parts of the world, every race, every colour, every creed, every religion, that we can all work together and get along, and I think it remains true today.”

Listen to the conversation here.

The US, a founding member of the UN, was a driving force behind the idea and the physical enactment of the organization.

US Ambassador Warren R. Austin, the chairman of the committee which was responsible for developing the UN campus said that the “United Nations is built upon principles that will outlast the steel and stone of any structure. The United Nations stands, held in God's law, as the foremost man-made instrument for the solution of problems and for unifying the peoples of the world.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the 80-year legacy of the United Nations, an institution dedicated to global peace, dignity, and equality. It details the UN's historical establishment, its multicultural staff, and its role in promoting human rights worldwide. The story emphasizes the broad and meaningful impact of the UN on international cooperation and human rights, with sufficient historical context and significance for the general public.
articles.personality_title: How New York Became Home to the United Nations’ Global Mission
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – After World War II, countries wanted to create a peaceful world. In 1945, they formed the United Nations (UN) to work together on peace, fairness, and human rights. The UN’s connection to New York City began soon after.

**What happened** – The UN first met in temporary places in New York, like Hunter College in The Bronx in 1946, where the Security Council held its first meeting on U.S. soil. Later, meetings moved to Lake Success on Long Island and Flushing Meadows in Queens. Finally, thanks to a gift from John D. Rockefeller Jr., the UN built its permanent headquarters in Manhattan, completed in 1952.

**Impact** – New York City became a symbol of international cooperation because it welcomed people from all over the world. The UN’s multicultural staff and global broadcasts helped spread important ideas like human rights. The city’s diversity matched the UN’s mission of bringing nations together to solve problems.

**What's next step** – The UN continues to grow, now with 193 member countries. Its headquarters in New York remains a place where world leaders meet to discuss peace and cooperation. The legacy of its early years helps guide its future work.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New York City’s role as the home of the United Nations shows how a city can welcome the world to work together for peace and equality.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment New York est devenue le siège de la mission mondiale des Nations Unies
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Après la Seconde Guerre mondiale, les pays ont voulu créer un monde pacifique. En 1945, ils ont formé les Nations Unies (ONU) pour travailler ensemble pour la paix, la justice et les droits humains. Le lien de l’ONU avec New York a commencé peu après.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’ONU s’est d’abord réunie dans des lieux temporaires à New York, comme Hunter College dans le Bronx en 1946, où le Conseil de sécurité a tenu sa première réunion sur le sol américain. Ensuite, les réunions ont eu lieu à Lake Success sur Long Island et Flushing Meadows dans Queens. Enfin, grâce à un don de John D. Rockefeller Jr., l’ONU a construit son siège permanent à Manhattan, terminé en 1952.

**Impact** – New York est devenue un symbole de coopération internationale car elle a accueilli des personnes du monde entier. Le personnel multiculturel de l’ONU et ses diffusions mondiales ont aidé à faire connaître des idées importantes comme les droits humains. La diversité de la ville reflète la mission de l’ONU de rassembler les nations.

**Prochaine étape** – L’ONU continue de grandir, avec maintenant 193 pays membres. Son siège à New York reste un lieu où les dirigeants mondiaux se réunissent pour discuter de paix et de coopération. L’héritage de ses premières années guide son travail futur.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le rôle de New York comme siège des Nations Unies montre comment une ville peut accueillir le monde pour travailler ensemble à la paix et à l’égalité.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo Nueva York se convirtió en el hogar de la misión global de las Naciones Unidas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Después de la Segunda Guerra Mundial, los países quisieron crear un mundo pacífico. En 1945, formaron las Naciones Unidas (ONU) para trabajar juntos por la paz, la justicia y los derechos humanos. La conexión de la ONU con Nueva York comenzó poco después.

**Qué pasó** – La ONU primero se reunió en lugares temporales en Nueva York, como Hunter College en El Bronx en 1946, donde el Consejo de Seguridad tuvo su primera reunión en suelo estadounidense. Luego, las reuniones se trasladaron a Lake Success en Long Island y Flushing Meadows en Queens. Finalmente, gracias a un donativo de John D. Rockefeller Jr., la ONU construyó su sede permanente en Manhattan, terminada en 1952.

**Impacto** – Nueva York se convirtió en un símbolo de cooperación internacional porque acogió a personas de todo el mundo. El personal multicultural de la ONU y sus transmisiones globales ayudaron a difundir ideas importantes como los derechos humanos. La diversidad de la ciudad reflejó la misión de la ONU de unir a las naciones.

**Próximo paso** – La ONU sigue creciendo, ahora con 193 países miembros. Su sede en Nueva York sigue siendo un lugar donde los líderes mundiales se reúnen para hablar de paz y cooperación. El legado de sus primeros años guía su trabajo futuro.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El papel de Nueva York como hogar de las Naciones Unidas muestra cómo una ciudad puede recibir al mundo para trabajar juntos por la paz y la igualdad.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of an open architectural blueprint merging into a stylized map of New York City’s boroughs, with symbolic elements like a vintage basketball court floor pattern and a radio microphone subtly integrated, all rendered in warm, natural tones to evoke unity, peace, and the historic journey of the United Nations establishing its global home.

articles.title: Japan Authorities Take Steps to Address Athlete Abuse
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Photo montage of the Tokyo Olympic Stadium and youth in Japan training and playing popular sports.
                    © 2019 imagenavi/Aflo; 2005 Doable/a.collectionRF/amanaimages; 2020 Human Rights Watch; 2015 Satoru Kobayashi/a.collectionRF/amanaimages; 2016 RYO/amanaimages; Trevor Williams/Getty Images; 2020 Human Rights Watch; 2016 Matsuo/Aflo; AdobeStock      
          
    


  
The Japan Sports Agency (JSA) recently released its Guidelines for Evaluating and Improving Safety Measures in Physical Activity and Sports (Trial Version). After documented suffering by Japanese athletes and insufficient mechanisms to report abuse, the government’s efforts to end mistreatment in sports are a positive step. However, effective implementation is crucial.The guidelines, published on January 27, 2026, come in five versions targeting groups involved in sports. The ones for athletes, coaches, and sports organizers, such as teams and clubs, include a section on abuse and harassment, provide examples of abusive behavior, identify contributing factors, and offer prevention and response measures.In 2020, Human Rights Watch documented systemic child abuse in Japanese sports. Five years later, the Basic Act on Sport was revised, requiring the national government to address abuse. Soon after, Human Rights Watch and advocacy partners wrote to the JSA commissioner, Junichi Kawai, recommending establishing a Safe Sport Act and a Safe Sport Center to ensure that athletes can report abuse and that their cases are addressed.On January 28, safe sport advocacy groups and former athletes and scholars met with the JSA to back efforts to eliminate harassment in sports and to stress the need for a Safe Sport Act and a Safe Sport Center.Carrying out the new guidelines is merely voluntary. The draft FY2026 budget states that JSA will promote the guidelines and create a program to register and publicize organizations that comply. However, the process to address abuses in organizations that have refused to carry out the suggested measures remains unclear. Japan needs a legal framework requiring sports organizations to act on abuse.While the guidelines list hotlines to report athlete abuse, each sports organization would operate its own complaint mechanism. Experts have raised concerns about insufficient staffing, funding, and expertise if organizations operate the hotlines. To establish a system that athletes can use without fear of retaliation and can trust to take effective action, an independent complaint mechanism is necessary.The Japanese government has embarked on a path to greater sports safety, but to succeed, effective implementation, including a Safe Sport Act and a Safe Sport Center, is needed so that athletes and children across Japan are protected.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Photo montage of the Tokyo Olympic Stadium and youth in Japan training and playing popular sports. © 2019 imagenavi/Aflo; 2005 Doable/a.collectionRF/amanaimages; 2020 Human Rights Watch; 2015 Satoru Kobayashi/a.collectionRF/amanaimages; 2016 RYO/amanaimages; Trevor Williams/Getty Images; 2020 Human Rights Watch; 2016 Matsuo/Aflo; AdobeStock

The Japan Sports Agency (JSA) recently released its Guidelines for Evaluating and Improving Safety Measures in Physical Activity and Sports (Trial Version). After documented suffering by Japanese athletes and insufficient mechanisms to report abuse, the government’s efforts to end mistreatment in sports are a positive step. However, effective implementation is crucial.

The guidelines, published on January 27, 2026, come in five versions targeting groups involved in sports. The ones for athletes, coaches, and sports organizers, such as teams and clubs, include a section on abuse and harassment, provide examples of abusive behavior, identify contributing factors, and offer prevention and response measures.

In 2020, Human Rights Watch documented systemic child abuse in Japanese sports. Five years later, the Basic Act on Sport was revised, requiring the national government to address abuse. Soon after, Human Rights Watch and advocacy partners wrote to the JSA commissioner, Junichi Kawai, recommending establishing a Safe Sport Act and a Safe Sport Center to ensure that athletes can report abuse and that their cases are addressed.

On January 28, safe sport advocacy groups and former athletes and scholars met with the JSA to back efforts to eliminate harassment in sports and to stress the need for a Safe Sport Act and a Safe Sport Center.

Carrying out the new guidelines is merely voluntary. The draft FY2026 budget states that JSA will promote the guidelines and create a program to register and publicize organizations that comply. However, the process to address abuses in organizations that have refused to carry out the suggested measures remains unclear. Japan needs a legal framework requiring sports organizations to act on abuse.

While the guidelines list hotlines to report athlete abuse, each sports organization would operate its own complaint mechanism. Experts have raised concerns about insufficient staffing, funding, and expertise if organizations operate the hotlines. To establish a system that athletes can use without fear of retaliation and can trust to take effective action, an independent complaint mechanism is necessary.

The Japanese government has embarked on a path to greater sports safety, but to succeed, effective implementation, including a Safe Sport Act and a Safe Sport Center, is needed so that athletes and children across Japan are protected.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant government initiative in Japan to address and prevent athlete abuse, a serious human rights issue affecting many people. The introduction of safety guidelines and advocacy for a Safe Sport Act and Center represent meaningful steps toward protecting athletes nationwide. While implementation challenges remain, the focus on systemic change and athlete protection has broad societal impact and substance.
articles.personality_title: Japan introduces new safety guidelines to protect athletes from abuse
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Japan, there have been problems with athletes facing abuse and harassment in sports. In 2020, a report showed many cases of child abuse in sports, leading the government to rethink how to keep athletes safe.

**What happened** – On January 27, 2026, the Japan Sports Agency (JSA) released new safety guidelines. These guidelines explain what abuse looks like, why it happens, and how to prevent it. They target athletes, coaches, and sports teams. The next day, groups supporting safe sports met with JSA to ask for stronger laws and a special center to help athletes report abuse.

**Impact** – This is important because it is the first time Japan has clear rules to protect athletes from harm. The guidelines give examples and advice, which can help sports groups understand and stop abuse. However, following the rules is voluntary for now, and there is no strong system to make sure all sports groups act on them. Experts say an independent way to report abuse is needed so athletes feel safe to speak up.

**What's next step** – The government plans to promote these guidelines and recognize sports groups that follow them. But Japan still needs a law, called the Safe Sport Act, and a Safe Sport Center to help athletes report problems without fear. These steps will help make sure the guidelines are used properly and protect athletes better.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Japan has taken a new step to protect athletes by creating safety guidelines, but stronger laws and support systems are needed to make sports safer for everyone.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Japon introduit de nouvelles directives pour protéger les athlètes contre les abus
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Au Japon, des athlètes ont été victimes d'abus et de harcèlement dans le sport. En 2020, un rapport a révélé de nombreux cas d'abus d'enfants dans le sport, ce qui a poussé le gouvernement à repenser la sécurité des athlètes.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le 27 janvier 2026, l'Agence japonaise du sport (JSA) a publié de nouvelles directives de sécurité. Ces directives expliquent ce qu'est l'abus, pourquoi il se produit et comment l'éviter. Elles s'adressent aux athlètes, entraîneurs et équipes sportives. Le lendemain, des groupes de défense du sport sain ont rencontré la JSA pour demander des lois plus fortes et un centre spécial pour aider les athlètes à signaler les abus.

**Impact** – C'est important car c'est la première fois que le Japon a des règles claires pour protéger les athlètes. Les directives donnent des exemples et des conseils, ce qui peut aider les groupes sportifs à comprendre et à arrêter les abus. Cependant, suivre ces règles est pour l'instant volontaire, et il n'existe pas de système fort pour s'assurer que tous les groupes sportifs les appliquent. Des experts disent qu'un moyen indépendant de signaler les abus est nécessaire pour que les athlètes puissent parler en toute sécurité.

**Prochaine étape** – Le gouvernement prévoit de promouvoir ces directives et de reconnaître les groupes sportifs qui les respectent. Mais le Japon doit encore créer une loi appelée « Safe Sport Act » et un centre Safe Sport pour aider les athlètes à signaler les problèmes sans crainte. Ces mesures aideront à garantir une meilleure protection des athlètes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Japon a fait un pas vers la protection des athlètes en créant des directives de sécurité, mais des lois plus fortes et des systèmes de soutien sont nécessaires pour rendre le sport plus sûr.
articles.personality_title_es: Japón presenta nuevas directrices para proteger a los atletas contra el abuso
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Japón, algunos atletas han sufrido abuso y acoso en el deporte. En 2020, un informe mostró muchos casos de abuso infantil en el deporte, lo que llevó al gobierno a reconsiderar cómo proteger a los atletas.

**Qué pasó** – El 27 de enero de 2026, la Agencia Deportiva de Japón (JSA) publicó nuevas directrices de seguridad. Estas explican qué es el abuso, por qué ocurre y cómo prevenirlo. Van dirigidas a atletas, entrenadores y equipos deportivos. Al día siguiente, grupos que apoyan el deporte seguro se reunieron con la JSA para pedir leyes más fuertes y un centro especial para ayudar a los atletas a denunciar abusos.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque es la primera vez que Japón tiene reglas claras para proteger a los atletas. Las directrices dan ejemplos y consejos que pueden ayudar a los grupos deportivos a entender y detener el abuso. Sin embargo, seguir estas reglas es voluntario por ahora, y no hay un sistema fuerte que asegure que todos los grupos deportivos las cumplan. Los expertos dicen que se necesita un mecanismo independiente para denunciar abusos y que los atletas puedan hablar con seguridad.

**Próximo paso** – El gobierno planea promover estas directrices y reconocer a los grupos deportivos que las sigan. Pero Japón aún necesita una ley llamada Safe Sport Act y un Centro Safe Sport para ayudar a los atletas a reportar problemas sin miedo. Estos pasos ayudarán a que las directrices se usen bien y protejan mejor a los atletas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Japón ha dado un nuevo paso para proteger a los atletas creando directrices de seguridad, pero se necesitan leyes más fuertes y sistemas de apoyo para hacer el deporte más seguro para todos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a large, sturdy traditional Japanese torii gate standing protectively over a group of young athletes represented by simple, colorful sports equipment—such as a soccer ball, baseball glove, and running shoes—arranged in a circle beneath it, with gentle light filtering through soft clouds, symbolizing safety, care, and the beginning of a new, hopeful era in sports.

articles.title: Forty-five years of progress after a key paper about the evolution of cooperation
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00802-7A 1981 publication showed how cooperators can prevail over defectors, laying the foundation for how the evolution of cooperation between unrelated individuals is studied.
articles.content:
Ever since Charles Darwin, scientists studying evolution have grappled with how cooperative behaviours that are costly to an individual but beneficial to others can be consistent with maximal fitness. In 1981, writing in Science, Robert Axelrod and William D. Hamilton1 presented key findings on how such cooperation can evolve. They accomplished this by weaving together two emerging ideas of the time in this area of research.

Nature 651, 894-895 (2026)

doi: https://doi.org/10.1038/d41586-026-00802-7

References Axelrod, R. & Hamilton, W. D. Science 211, 1390–1396 (1981). Maynard Smith, J. & Price, G. R. Nature 246, 15–18 (1973). Trivers, R. L. Q. Rev. Biol. 46, 35–57 (1971). Hamilton, W. D. J. Theor. Biol. 7, 1–16 (1964). Rapoport, A. & Chammah, A. M. Prisoner’s Dilemma: A Study in Conflict and Cooperation (Univ. Michigan Press, 1965). Hammerstein, P. in Genetic and Cultural Evolution of Cooperation (ed. Hammerstein, P.) 83–93 (MIT Press, 2003). Clutton-Brock, T. Nature 462, 51–57 (2009). Wu, J. & Axelrod, R. J. Conflict Resolut. 39, 183–189 (1995). Sugden, R. The Economics of Rights, Co-operation and Welfare (Blackwell, 1986). Lenfesty, H., Mathew, S., Fikes, T., Ross, C. T. & Boyd, R. Proc. R. Soc. B 291, 20240861 (2024). van Veelen, M., García, J., Rand, D. G. & Nowak, M. A. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 109, 9929–9934 (2012). Graser, C. & van Veelen, M. Tinbergen Institute Discussion Paper No. TI 2024-022/I (2024). Mathew, S. & Boyd, R. Preprint at PsyArXiv https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/e5zuc_v1 (2026). Hruschka, D. J. & Henrich, J. J. Theor. Biol. 239, 1–15 (2006). Leimar, O. & Bshary, R. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 121, e2317736121 (2024). Xue, M. & Silk, J. B. Evol. Hum. Behav. 33, 17–25 (2012). Download references

Competing Interests The authors declare no competing interests.

Related Articles

Subjects
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a foundational scientific breakthrough explaining the evolution of cooperation, which has broad implications for understanding social behavior and promoting cooperative human interactions. This progress has meaningful societal impact by informing how cooperation can prevail over selfishness, benefiting communities and human relations globally. The article is focused on a significant, well-documented scientific achievement with substantial context.
articles.personality_title: How a 1981 study changed our understanding of cooperation
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered how people or animals can work together even when helping others might cost them something. This idea is important because cooperation helps groups survive and succeed.

**What happened** – In 1981, researchers Robert Axelrod and William D. Hamilton published a study showing how cooperative behavior can develop and last, even among individuals who are not related. They combined ideas from game theory and biology to explain how cooperation can beat selfishness.

**Impact** – This study helped scientists understand why cooperation exists in nature and human society. It showed that cooperation can be a winning strategy, which changes how we think about social behavior and relationships.

**What's next step** – Researchers continue to build on this work to explore cooperation in different species and cultures. This knowledge can help improve teamwork, conflict resolution, and community building in real life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A key 1981 study explained how cooperation can succeed over selfishness, shaping how we understand social behavior today.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment une étude de 1981 a changé notre compréhension de la coopération
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se sont longtemps demandé comment les personnes ou les animaux peuvent coopérer même lorsque cela leur coûte quelque chose. Cette idée est importante car la coopération aide les groupes à survivre et à réussir.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – En 1981, les chercheurs Robert Axelrod et William D. Hamilton ont publié une étude montrant comment un comportement coopératif peut se développer et durer, même entre individus non apparentés. Ils ont combiné des idées de la théorie des jeux et de la biologie pour expliquer comment la coopération peut l'emporter sur l'égoïsme.

**Impact** – Cette étude a aidé les scientifiques à comprendre pourquoi la coopération existe dans la nature et la société humaine. Elle a montré que la coopération peut être une stratégie gagnante, ce qui change notre vision du comportement social et des relations.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continuent de s’appuyer sur ce travail pour explorer la coopération chez différentes espèces et cultures. Ces connaissances peuvent aider à améliorer le travail d’équipe, la résolution des conflits et la construction de communautés dans la vie réelle.

**En résumé** – Une étude clé de 1981 a expliqué comment la coopération peut réussir face à l’égoïsme, façonnant notre compréhension du comportement social aujourd’hui.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo un estudio de 1981 cambió nuestra comprensión de la cooperación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han preguntado durante mucho tiempo cómo las personas o animales pueden cooperar incluso cuando ayudar a otros les cuesta algo. Esta idea es importante porque la cooperación ayuda a los grupos a sobrevivir y tener éxito.

**Qué pasó** – En 1981, los investigadores Robert Axelrod y William D. Hamilton publicaron un estudio que mostró cómo el comportamiento cooperativo puede desarrollarse y mantenerse, incluso entre individuos que no están relacionados. Combinaron ideas de la teoría de juegos y la biología para explicar cómo la cooperación puede superar al egoísmo.

**Impacto** – Este estudio ayudó a los científicos a entender por qué la cooperación existe en la naturaleza y en la sociedad humana. Demostró que la cooperación puede ser una estrategia ganadora, lo que cambia nuestra forma de pensar sobre el comportamiento social y las relaciones.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores continúan ampliando este trabajo para explorar la cooperación en diferentes especies y culturas. Este conocimiento puede ayudar a mejorar el trabajo en equipo, la resolución de conflictos y la construcción de comunidades en la vida real.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un estudio clave de 1981 explicó cómo la cooperación puede triunfar sobre el egoísmo, moldeando nuestra comprensión del comportamiento social hoy en día.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a diverse group of animals—such as birds, ants, and meerkats—engaged in cooperative activities like sharing food and grooming each other within a harmonious natural setting, rendered in soft, natural earth tones and gentle light, symbolizing the evolution of cooperation through mutual benefit and collective fitness.
